result:
I keep thinking about the shelf in my grandmother’s hallway that was devoted to half-used bars of soap.  Not special soap—no artisanal scents or pressed flecks of lavender—just the plain white hotel bars she rescued from every trip, wrapped in crumpled tissue and ferried home like small, contraband animals.  Over the decades they grew brittle, their edges sharpening into tiny tectonic plates.  When you lifted one, it left a pale scar of dust on the veneered wood, a ghost rectangle that testified to how long it had been resting there, waiting for a future hand to require it.  I used to stand in front of that shelf, nine or ten years old, trying to decide whether the older soaps were more valuable because they had waited so patiently, or less valuable because they had grown strange, suspect, possibly contagious with age.  I never solved the riddle; I only knew the assortment embarrassed me whenever friends came over.  I wanted to explain: she survived the Depression, she saves string, she can still darn a sock heel into a seamless lattice.  But the words would have sounded like apology, and children hate apologizing for the people they love.

Now, thirty years later, I find myself doing the same thing with notebooks.  I buy them faster than I can fill them, and when one contains only seven or eight pages of scrawl—an overheard phrase, a dream about tidal waves, the name of a medication I promised to research—I retire it to a drawer as if it has served nobly and deserves its rest.  The drawer is becoming a pension fund for prematurely aging paper.  Last week I tipped it open and discovered strata: marbled covers from college, a tiny bright Moleskine bought in an airport that still smells like pretzel fog, a soft gray notebook with a debossed owl whose eyes exactly match the metallic ink I used one winter to record the progress of a single depressive episode.  The combined weight was startling, like holding a brick composed entirely of abandoned intentions.  I told myself I would return to those fragments, stitch them into something larger, the way my grandmother used to seam two soap slivers into one puck by wetting and pressing them overnight.  But I won’t.  The energy required to re-inhabit a moment once the chemical composition of the self has changed is more than I can reliably summon.  Still, I can’t discard them.  That would be murder of a potential I once believed in fiercely enough to purchase.

Sometimes I wonder if the obsession with preservation is a Protestant reflex, a fear that somewhere a cosmic auditor tallies every wasted crumb of experience.  I was raised on parables about talents buried in the ground, about virgins whose lamps ran out of oil.  The lesson was always: use what you are given, and use it now, because the bridegroom is coming, the master is returning, the night is approaching when no one can work.  I internalized the urgency but not the clarity; I hoard possibility the way others hoard money or calories or affection.  The drawer of notebooks is my private museum of almost.  The soaps in my grandmother’s hallway were her museum of rescue.  We both believed, without saying so, that someday the world would narrow to the size of what we had saved, and then the saved things would save us back.

On the day she died, we opened her linen closet and found grocery bags stuffed with those tiny plastic tables that prevent pizza boxes from collapsing onto the cheese.  She washed them, stacked them in towers of thirty, tied each tower with frayed ribbon.  No one in the family had ever seen her do this; it must have been a solitary ritual, maybe performed in the hush of 3 a.m. insomnia.  My cousins laughed—sharp, startled barks—because the discovery was too sad to absorb without a joke.  I took one tower home and put it in my own drawer, beside the notebooks.  I have never once reused a pizza table, but when I glimpse their tiny tripod shadows, I feel the same complicated tenderness I feel for the soaps: here is proof that she was here, that her hands moved through the same rooms I move through, that she believed tomorrow might require the artifacts of today.

There is a neurological term—prospective memory—that describes the ability to remember to perform an action in the future.  Take meds at 4, call sister on her birthday, water the fern before vacation.  Prospective memory is fragile; it depends on cues, on the world remaining legible.  When I think about my notebooks, my grandmother’s soaps, the pizza tables, I realize we were both building external prosthetics for prospective memory.  We were arranging cues that would prompt tomorrow’s self to act: write, wash, feed someone, survive.  The fact that tomorrow keeps becoming today and then yesterday faster than we can update the cues is a mechanical problem, not a moral one, but it feels like sin.  It feels like we were judged and found wasteful, when really we were just human, which is to say temporary, which is to say always on the verge of evacuation.

I read once that soap is made of fat and lye, two substances that alone will destroy you but together create cleansing.  I think about that whenever I wash my hands after slicing onions, how the bar slips like a small tame animal, how it keeps diminishing, carrying away particles of me I will never meet again.  Maybe that is the only afterlife we get: to be suspended in the runoff, briefly illuminated by bathroom light, then gone into pipes, into rivers, into the great salt body that keeps score of every mile we never traveled, every sentence we never finished.  And still the bar sits in the dish, smaller but intact, waiting for the next use, the next rescue, the next small daily forgiveness.

Last night I opened a fresh notebook—crimson cover, uncreased spine—and wrote on the very first page: “This is the year I stop stockpiling futures.”  I felt heroic for approximately four minutes, until I realized I had no plan for the remaining blank pages.  The conviction drained like bathwater, leaving a faint ring around the edges of the moment.  I sat there listening to the apartment settle, the fridge humming its one-note lullaby, the upstairs neighbor pacing a pattern I have by heart.  Eventually I tore out the page, folded it into a tiny paper table, and balanced it on top of the stack from my grandmother.  It wobbled but stayed upright.  I imagine someone finding it someday—my niece, a stranger, maybe just the recycling truck’s magnetic mouth—and wondering what meal it once protected, what steam it rose through, what hunger it served.  They will never know, and that anonymity feels like mercy.  The thing is no longer mine; it is just evidence that once, for a few seconds, I tried to build a small architecture against collapse, and the architecture held long enough to be carried away.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
